summary: This PR adds support for setting an explicit multipart boundary via the Content-Type header, introducing `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` in `_multipart.py` and wiring it into `_models.py`. The implementation is broadly correct for typical inputs, but there are meaningful security concerns around missing validation of the user-supplied boundary value, and a handful of correctness edge-cases in the RFC-compliant parsing of quoted-string boundary values. Test coverage of the new helper function's edge-case branches is also incomplete. (1 finding(s) below the 0.4 confidence threshold hidden.)

findings.title: User-supplied boundary used without RFC 2046 validation — multipart injection and DoS risk
findings.detail:
The boundary string extracted from the caller-supplied Content-Type header is used verbatim as the multipart delimiter (e.g. `b"--%s\r\n" % self.boundary`) and decoded directly with `boundary.decode('ascii')` (line ~199) without any validation or error handling.

**Two distinct risks:**
1. **Multipart injection:** RFC 2046 §5.1.1 restricts boundary characters to a specific safe set. No validation is performed, so an attacker who controls the Content-Type header can craft a boundary containing `\r\n` or `--` sequences, breaking the multipart body structure and potentially smuggling additional form fields or bypassing server-side validation.
2. **Denial of service / unhandled exception:** If the boundary contains non-ASCII bytes, `boundary.decode('ascii')` raises an unhandled `UnicodeDecodeError`, crashing the request-building path. The `os.urandom` fallback is only used when `boundary is None`, so a non-None but non-ASCII boundary bypasses it entirely.

**Recommendation:** After extraction in `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type`, validate the returned bytes against the RFC 2046 boundary character set (bchars: alphanumerics + `'()+_,-./:=?` and space, max 70 chars) and return `None` (triggering the safe random-boundary fallback) if validation fails. Alternatively, wrap `boundary.decode('ascii')` in a `try/except UnicodeDecodeError` and fall back to a random boundary.

findings.title: Splitting on `;` breaks quoted-string boundary values containing semicolons
findings.detail:
The parser splits the content-type header on `b";"` before extracting the boundary parameter. RFC 2046 allows the boundary parameter value to be a quoted-string, which can theoretically contain a semicolon (e.g. `boundary="ab;cd"`). Splitting on `;` would break this into `b'boundary="ab'` and `b'cd"'`, causing the parser to fail to recognise the boundary and silently fall back to a random one.

While semicolons inside boundary values are rare in practice, this is a correctness gap relative to the RFC. A proper implementation should parse quoted-string parameter values before splitting.

findings.title: Missing unit tests for `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` edge-case branches
findings.detail:
Several branches of the new helper are not directly tested:
1. `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type(None)` — the `not content_type` early-return.
2. A non-multipart content-type (e.g. `b"application/json"`) — the `not content_type.startswith(b"multipart/form-data")` early-return.
3. `b"multipart/form-data"` with no semicolon at all — the `if b";" in content_type` guard is False, falling through to `return None`.

While the integration tests cover the happy path, direct unit tests of these branches would pin the contract of the standalone helper and prevent regressions.

findings.title: test_multipart_header_without_boundary does not verify the auto-generated boundary was used in the body
findings.detail: When no boundary is present in the Content-Type header, the code falls back to a random boundary. `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` only checks the HTTP status code and that the Content-Type header equals the user-supplied value; it does not assert that a valid multipart body was actually sent using an auto-generated boundary. The no-boundary fallback behaviour is therefore not fully covered.

findings.title: No test for explicit multipart boundary via direct `httpx.Request()` construction
findings.detail: All new tests exercise the boundary-extraction logic through `httpx.Client`. The `Request` constructor path in `_models.py` lines 336–347 is not tested independently with an explicit `content-type` boundary header supplied at construction time, leaving that code path without direct coverage.

findings.title: Boundary quoted-string stripping with `strip(b'"')` silently truncates or mishandles edge-case values
findings.detail:
The boundary value is extracted with `section.strip()[len(b"boundary="):].strip(b'"')`. The `bytes.strip(chars)` method removes ALL leading and trailing characters in the given set, not just one quote on each side.

This causes two edge cases:
1. A boundary value that is exactly one or more double-quote characters (e.g. `boundary=""`) — while technically invalid per RFC 2046, a user or test could supply it — would be stripped to an empty bytes object instead of a meaningful error.
2. A quoted boundary with internal leading/trailing whitespace (e.g. `boundary="+++ "`) produces `b'+++ '` (with a trailing space) rather than `b'+++'`, because only the quotes are stripped, not internal whitespace. This means the actual boundary used in the body differs from what the Content-Type header advertises, causing a parse mismatch in the receiving server.

No tests cover these edge cases (see also f18).

findings.title: MultipartStream reconstructs Content-Type internally but user-supplied header is preserved via setdefault — behaviour is correct but fragile
findings.detail:
**Reviewer disagreement.** One reviewer flagged that `MultipartStream.__init__` always rebuilds `self.content_type` from the extracted boundary, potentially overwriting the user's original Content-Type. However, `_prepare()` uses `headers.setdefault()`, so the user-supplied header wins and the reconstructed value is only a fallback.

This is correct behaviour as long as `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` returns the right boundary, but the round-trip (user header → extract boundary → reconstruct header → setdefault) is fragile: any deviation in formatting between the user-supplied and reconstructed values is silently hidden. If the extraction ever returns a wrong boundary, the body and header will disagree without any visible indication. Consider asserting or logging when the extracted boundary is used.

findings.title: Boundary is unconditionally extracted from Content-Type even when `files` is None
findings.detail: The boundary is parsed from the Content-Type header regardless of whether `files` is provided. When `files=None` and only `data=` is given, the extracted boundary is silently discarded and `encode_urlencoded_data` is called instead. This is harmless in terms of correctness but can create a confusing situation: a user who passes `content-type: multipart/form-data; boundary=foo` with `data=` only (no files) will get a URL-encoded body while the Content-Type header claims multipart. This is a pre-existing design issue made slightly more visible by the PR.

findings.title: Case-insensitive boundary= prefix check is correct but implementation is fragile
findings.detail: The check `section.strip().lower().startswith(b"boundary=")` correctly handles mixed-case keys like `Boundary=` or `BOUNDARY=` because `len(b"boundary=")` equals `len(b"BOUNDARY=")` (9 bytes either way), so the subsequent slice on the non-lowercased `section.strip()` always skips the right number of bytes. Multiple reviewers independently flagged this as a potential bug but confirmed it is actually correct. It is noted here for robustness — the implementation relies on a non-obvious coincidence of byte lengths rather than explicit handling.

findings.title: Outer `if b";" in content_type` guard is redundant
findings.detail: The `for section in content_type.split(b";"):` loop already handles the no-semicolon case — it yields one element (the whole value), which will not match `startswith(b"boundary=")` for a plain `multipart/form-data` value. The extra guard adds indentation and complexity without benefit, deviating from the flat style favoured elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: Magic constant `len(b"boundary=")` duplicated — prefer a named constant
findings.detail: The byte literal `b"boundary="` appears twice: once in the `startswith` check and once inside `len(...)` for the slice. Extracting a named constant (e.g. `_BOUNDARY_PREFIX = b"boundary="`) would eliminate the duplication, make the intent clear, and be consistent with how other modules in the codebase centralise magic strings.

findings.title: Inline comment uses lower-case first letter — inconsistent with surrounding style
findings.detail: The comment `# parse boundary according to` starts with a lowercase letter. Surrounding comments in the file (e.g. `# This large tuple based API largely mirrors requests' API`) use sentence-case. Consider changing to `# Parse boundary according to ...`.

findings.title: test_multipart_header_without_boundary omits body-content assertion present in sibling tests
findings.detail: Every other parametrised multipart test (e.g. `test_multipart`, `test_multipart_explicit_boundary`) asserts `multipart["file"] == [b"<file content>"]` to verify the body was encoded correctly. This test only checks status code and Content-Type, making coverage asymmetric and potentially masking regressions in body encoding.

findings.title: Parametrize style inconsistent within the file
findings.detail: Existing tests use `@pytest.mark.parametrize(("key"), ...)` while the new tests at lines 45 and 79 use the single-string form `@pytest.mark.parametrize("header", [...])`. The single-string form is actually the preferred pytest style, but the inconsistency within the file is a readability concern that should be resolved (ideally by updating the older tests).

findings.title: Boundary value parsing strips only leading/trailing double-quotes, mishandling trailing whitespace and quoted values
findings.detail:
The boundary is extracted with `section.strip()[len(b"boundary="):].strip(b'"')`. When a header like `boundary=+++ ;` is split by `;`, the section `b" boundary=+++ "` is stripped to `b"boundary=+++"`, then sliced to `b"+++"` — that works. But for `boundary="+++" ;`, the section is stripped to `b'boundary="+++"'`, sliced to `b'"+++"'`, then `strip(b'"')` gives `b'+++'` — also OK.

However, `str.strip(chars)` strips ALL leading AND trailing characters in the given char-set, not just one. So a boundary value that legitimately starts or ends with a double-quote character inside the value (which RFC 2046 technically disallows in the token form but allows in the quoted-string form) would be silently truncated. More importantly, the trailing-whitespace case (`boundary=+++ ;`) yields the section `b" boundary=+++ "`, which after `.strip()` is `b"boundary=+++"` — so `[9:]` is `b"+++"` and `.strip(b'"')` is `b"+++"`. That's fine.

The real edge-case bug: if a value is `boundary="` (a boundary consisting of just a double-quote), `strip(b'"')` would return an empty bytes object instead of `b'"'`. The `strip(chars)` removes ALL occurrences from both ends, so `b'"""'.strip(b'"')` returns `b''`. This is a minor correctness issue for RFC-legal quoted-string boundaries that contain the delimiter character at their edges.

More critically: if the boundary is quoted AND also has a trailing space inside the quote, e.g. `boundary="+++ "`, parsing produces `b'+++ '` (with a trailing space) rather than `b'+++'` as specified, because only quotes are stripped, not internal whitespace. This means the actual boundary used in the body would differ from what the content-type says, causing a parse mismatch.

findings.title: MultipartStream overwrites Content-Type with auto-generated value, ignoring the user-supplied boundary in the content-type header
findings.detail:
When a user provides an explicit `Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=+++` header, the code correctly extracts `+++` as the boundary and passes it to `MultipartStream`. However, `MultipartStream.__init__` always reconstructs its own `self.content_type` string as:

```python
self.content_type = "multipart/form-data; boundary=%s" % boundary.decode("ascii")
```

Then `get_headers()` returns `{"Content-Type": self.content_type}`, and `_prepare()` calls `self.headers.setdefault(key, value)` for the Content-Type. Since the user already set `content-type` in `self.headers`, `setdefault` will NOT overwrite it — so the user's original content-type string is preserved.

This means the content-type header the user provided is kept intact (good), while the body is encoded with the boundary extracted from it (also good). This actually works correctly as long as `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` returns the right boundary value. No bug here, but see the parsing issues above.

findings.title: boundary is extracted from content-type even when `files` is None, causing unnecessary parsing
findings.detail: The boundary is unconditionally extracted from the content-type header regardless of whether `files` is provided. In `encode_request`, the `boundary` parameter is only used when `files` is truthy (line 210: `elif files:`). If `files` is None and data is provided as a dict, `encode_urlencoded_data` is called instead, ignoring `boundary` entirely. This is harmless in terms of correctness but means that if a user passes `content-type: multipart/form-data; boundary=foo` with `data=` only (no files), the boundary is silently parsed and discarded — the content type header is also kept in the request as-is, conflicting with the actual urlencoded body. This is a pre-existing design issue not introduced by this PR, but the PR now makes it slightly more confusing.

findings.title: Boundary parsing does case-insensitive comparison but case-sensitive slice, so mixed-case `Boundary=` is parsed incorrectly
findings.detail: The check `section.strip().lower().startswith(b"boundary=")` is case-insensitive (e.g. matches `Boundary=foo`), but the slice `section.strip()[len(b"boundary="):]` is applied to the ORIGINAL (non-lowercased) bytes. This means if the content-type has `Boundary=foo`, the check passes, but then `section.strip()` is e.g. `b"Boundary=foo"` and `[9:]` correctly gives `b"foo"` (since `len("boundary=") == len("Boundary=") == 9`). So this is actually fine — `len(b"boundary=")` is always 9 regardless of case, and the slice length is correct. No bug here.

findings.title: split(b";") does not handle semicolons inside quoted boundary values
findings.detail: RFC 2046 allows the boundary parameter value to be a quoted-string, which could theoretically contain a semicolon (e.g. `boundary="ab;cd"`). Splitting on `;` would break such a value into `b'boundary="ab'` and `b'cd"'`, causing the parser to fail to recognise the boundary. While semicolons in boundary values are rare in practice, this is a correctness issue relative to the RFC spec.

findings.title: Content-Type header encoded with `self.headers.encoding` which may not match the encoding used when the header was stored
findings.detail: `content_type.encode(self.headers.encoding)` re-encodes the already-decoded string value of the content-type header using `self.headers.encoding`. If the headers encoding is something other than ASCII/UTF-8, this could produce incorrect bytes passed to `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type`. In practice, content-type header values are always ASCII, so this is unlikely to cause a real problem, but it is a subtle dependency on the internal encoding state of the `Headers` object.

findings.title: Boundary value injected into multipart body without RFC 2046 character validation
findings.detail:
The boundary string extracted from the caller-supplied Content-Type header is used verbatim as the multipart delimiter (lines 220/223 of _multipart.py: `b"--%s\r\n" % self.boundary`). RFC 2046 §5.1.1 restricts boundary characters to a specific safe set (alphanumerics plus a handful of punctuation). No validation of this constraint is performed before the extracted value is used.

An attacker who controls the Content-Type header (e.g. a value is passed through from an untrusted source) could craft a boundary that:
1. Contains `\r\n` or `--` sequences, breaking the structure of the multipart body and potentially smuggling additional form fields or injecting content that bypasses server-side validation.
2. Contains non-ASCII bytes that later cause a UnicodeDecodeError in `MultipartStream.__init__` at `boundary.decode('ascii')` — a denial-of-service vector.

Recommendation: after extraction in `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type`, validate the returned bytes against the RFC 2046 boundary character set (BNF: `bchars = bcharsnospace / SPACE`, `bcharsnospace = DIGIT / ALPHA / "'" / "(" / ")" / "+" / "_" / "," / "-" / "." / "/" / ":" / "=" / "?"`, max 70 chars) and return `None` (falling back to a random boundary) if validation fails.

findings.title: Case-insensitive boundary= prefix match is incomplete — only the prefix is lowercased, not the full token
findings.detail: The comparison `section.strip().lower().startswith(b"boundary=")` lowercases the whole section before the `startswith` check, but then slices the original (non-lowercased) bytes with `section.strip()[len(b"boundary="):]`. This is correct because only the key name is compared case-insensitively and the value is returned as-is — but it means a key written as `Boundary=` or `BOUNDARY=` passes the `startswith` check yet the slice always skips exactly 9 bytes (`len(b"boundary=")`), which works correctly since the length of `boundary=` and `BOUNDARY=` is the same. This is not a bug per se, but the implementation is fragile. More importantly, because the comparison is done after `.lower()` but the slice is done on the non-lowercased version via `section.strip()`, if `section.strip()` starts with mixed-case `Boundary=somevalue`, the slice `section.strip()[9:]` correctly extracts `somevalue`. No security issue here beyond the one described in the main finding above, but worth noting for robustness.

findings.title: User-supplied boundary decoded as ASCII without error handling — potential DoS / unhandled exception
findings.detail:
In `MultipartStream.__init__`, line 199: `boundary.decode('ascii')`. When a user-supplied boundary (extracted from the Content-Type header) contains non-ASCII bytes, this raises an unhandled `UnicodeDecodeError`, crashing the request-building code path. An adversary (or buggy middleware) that can set an arbitrary Content-Type header with a non-ASCII boundary value can trigger this crash.

The `binascii.hexlify(os.urandom(16))` fallback is only used when `boundary is None`, so a non-None but non-ASCII boundary bypasses the safe fallback.

Recommendation: either validate in `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` that the extracted boundary is ASCII-safe (or restrict to the RFC 2046 character set), or add a `try/except UnicodeDecodeError` around the `.decode('ascii')` call and fall back to a random boundary.

findings.title: Inline comment should start with a capital letter and end without a period — inconsistent with surrounding style
findings.detail: The comment `# parse boundary according to` starts lowercase and has no trailing period. The surrounding code (e.g. `# This large tuple based API largely mirror's requests' API`) and the rest of the codebase use sentence-case for inline comments. Consider: `# Parse boundary according to`.

findings.title: Magic constant `len(b"boundary=")` — prefer a named variable for clarity
findings.detail: The slice `section.strip()[len(b"boundary=") :]` embeds the byte literal twice (once in the `startswith` check on line 32 and once here). Extracting a named constant, e.g. `_BOUNDARY_PREFIX = b"boundary="`, would remove the duplication and make the intent obvious — consistent with how other modules in this codebase centralise magic strings.

findings.title: Outer `if b";" in content_type` guard is redundant
findings.detail: The `for section in content_type.split(b";"):` loop already handles the case where there is no `;` — it simply yields one element (the whole value), which will not match `startswith(b"boundary=")` unless the entire value is the boundary token. The extra guard adds indentation and complexity without adding value, deviating from the simple/flat style favoured elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` does not assert body content — inconsistent with sibling tests
findings.detail: Every other parametrised multipart test (e.g. `test_multipart`, `test_multipart_explicit_boundary`) also asserts `multipart["file"] == [b"<file content>"]` to verify the body was encoded correctly. This test only checks status code and Content-Type, which makes the coverage asymmetric and may mask regressions. While this is a test-quality observation rather than a style bug, the inconsistency in pattern is a readability concern.

findings.title: Parametrize style inconsistent with rest of the file
findings.detail: The existing tests use `@pytest.mark.parametrize(("key"), ...)` (pre-existing, untouched line 97), but the new tests at lines 45 and 79 use `@pytest.mark.parametrize("header", [...])` — a single string rather than a tuple. While the single-string form is actually the preferred pytest style, it does differ from the pattern in the surrounding unchanged tests, creating an inconsistency within the file.

findings.title: No test for get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type with content_type=None
findings.detail: The function returns None immediately when content_type is None (first branch of the `not content_type or ...` check). While this path is exercised indirectly through the models integration, there is no direct unit test of get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type(None) to pin the contract of the standalone helper.

findings.title: No test for get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type with a non-multipart content-type
findings.detail: The early-return branch `not content_type.startswith(b"multipart/form-data")` (e.g. passing b"application/json") is never exercised by any test. A direct unit test of the helper with a non-multipart content-type string would close this gap.

findings.title: No test for bare 'multipart/form-data' content-type (no semicolon at all)
findings.detail: The branch `if b";" in content_type` is False when the header is exactly b"multipart/form-data" with no parameters whatsoever. None of the parametrised cases in test_multipart_header_without_boundary cover this — both supplied cases contain a semicolon. This path falls through to `return None` and is untested.

findings.title: No test for explicit multipart boundary passed via httpx.Request() directly (not via Client)
findings.detail: All new tests exercise the boundary-extraction logic through httpx.Client. The Request constructor path (lines 336-347 of _models.py) is not tested independently with an explicit boundary header supplied at construction time, leaving the direct Request(..., headers={"content-type": ...}) code path without explicit coverage.

findings.title: No test for boundary value surrounded by double-quotes that also contains whitespace
findings.detail: The strip logic is `section.strip()[len(b"boundary="):].strip(b'"')`. A quoted boundary with leading/trailing spaces inside the quotes (e.g. `boundary=" +++ "`) is not tested; it's unclear whether the current implementation handles this correctly and no test guards the behavior.

findings.title: test_multipart_header_without_boundary does not verify that a random boundary was generated
findings.detail: When no boundary is present in the content-type header the code falls back to a random boundary. The test only checks the status code and that the Content-Type header equals the user-supplied value, but does not assert that a valid multipart body was actually sent (i.e. that the stream used some auto-generated boundary). This means the no-boundary fallback behaviour is not fully covered.

findings.detail:
The boundary string extracted from the caller-supplied Content-Type header is used verbatim as the multipart delimiter (e.g. `b"--%s\r\n" % self.boundary`) and decoded directly with `boundary.decode('ascii')` (line ~199) without any validation or error handling.

**Two distinct risks:**
1. **Multipart injection:** RFC 2046 §5.1.1 restricts boundary characters to a specific safe set. No validation is performed, so an attacker who controls the Content-Type header can craft a boundary containing `\r\n` or `--` sequences, breaking the multipart body structure and potentially smuggling additional form fields or bypassing server-side validation.
2. **Denial of service / unhandled exception:** If the boundary contains non-ASCII bytes, `boundary.decode('ascii')` raises an unhandled `UnicodeDecodeError`, crashing the request-building path. **Recommendation:** After extraction in `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type`, validate the returned bytes against the RFC 2046 boundary character set (bchars: alphanumerics + `'()+_,-./:=?` and space, max 70 chars) and return `None` (triggering the safe random-boundary fallback) if validation fails. Alternatively, wrap `boundary.decode('ascii')` in a `try/except UnicodeDecodeError` and fall back to a random boundary.

findings.detail:
The parser splits the content-type header on `b";"` before extracting the boundary parameter. findings.title: Missing unit tests for `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` edge-case branches
findings.detail:
Several branches of the new helper are not directly tested:
1. `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type(None)` — the `not content_type` early-return.
2. A non-multipart content-type (e.g. `b"application/json"`) — the `not content_type.startswith(b"multipart/form-data")` early-return.
3. `b"multipart/form-data"` with no semicolon at all — the `if b";" in content_type` guard is False, falling through to `return None`.

While the integration tests cover the happy path, direct unit tests of these branches would pin the contract of the standalone helper and prevent regressions.

findings.detail: When no boundary is present in the Content-Type header, the code falls back to a random boundary. `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` only checks the HTTP status code and that the Content-Type header equals the user-supplied value; it does not assert that a valid multipart body was actually sent using an auto-generated boundary. The no-boundary fallback behaviour is therefore not fully covered.

The extra guard adds indentation and complexity without benefit, deviating from the flat style favoured elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: Magic constant `len(b"boundary=")` duplicated — prefer a named constant
findings.detail: The byte literal `b"boundary="` appears twice: once in the `startswith` check and once inside `len(...)` for the slice. Extracting a named constant (e.g. `_BOUNDARY_PREFIX = b"boundary="`) would eliminate the duplication, make the intent clear, and be consistent with how other modules in the codebase centralise magic strings.

findings.title: Inline comment uses lower-case first letter — inconsistent with surrounding style
findings.detail: The comment `# parse boundary according to` starts with a lowercase letter. Surrounding comments in the file (e.g. `# This large tuple based API largely mirrors requests' API`) use sentence-case. Consider changing to `# Parse boundary according to ...`.

findings.title: test_multipart_header_without_boundary omits body-content assertion present in sibling tests
findings.detail: Every other parametrised multipart test (e.g. `test_multipart`, `test_multipart_explicit_boundary`) asserts `multipart["file"] == [b"<file content>"]` to verify the body was encoded correctly. This test only checks status code and Content-Type, making coverage asymmetric and potentially masking regressions in body encoding.

findings.title: Parametrize style inconsistent within the file
findings.detail: Existing tests use `@pytest.mark.parametrize(("key"), ...)` while the new tests at lines 45 and 79 use the single-string form `@pytest.mark.parametrize("header", [...])`. The single-string form is actually the preferred pytest style, but the inconsistency within the file is a readability concern that should be resolved (ideally by updating the older tests).
